title: Iterable and iterator protocols

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial §§4.7 and 5.1-5.6: iteration, range, comprehensions, and sequence techniques; atomic topic: Iterable and iterator protocols
sources.claim: An iterable can produce an iterator, while an iterator is a stateful one-pass object whose __next__ yields values until it raises StopIteration.

sources.locator: The Python Language Reference; Python 3.14; Data Model iterator protocol plus yield expressions, generator functions, and decorators; atomic topic: Iterable and iterator protocols
sources.claim: An iterable can produce an iterator, while an iterator is a stateful one-pass object whose __next__ yields values until it raises StopIteration.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An iterable can produce an iterator, while an iterator is a stateful one-pass object whose __next__ yields values until it raises StopIteration.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A custom Countdown iterable returns a new iterator for each for loop, letting two traversals progress independently. Your goal is to trace iteration state and implement a small iterable or iterator correctly. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Core collections and text; Functions, scope and recursion; Representation, equality and container protocols; for, while and loop control. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: An iterable can produce an iterator, while an iterator is a stateful one-pass object whose __next__ yields values until it raises StopIteration. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: iter(iterator) normally returns itself, exhausted iterators stay exhausted, multiple iterators may or may not be independent, mutation can invalidate traversal, and StopIteration has special generator rules. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Iterable and iterator are interchangeable, and calling iter on an exhausted iterator restarts it.

includes: Distinguish reusable iterables from stateful one-pass iterators.
Trace iteration state and implement a small iterable or iterator correctly.
excludes: iter(iterator) normally returns itself, exhausted iterators stay exhausted, multiple iterators may or may not be independent, mutation can invalidate traversal, and StopIteration has special generator rules.

outcomes: Trace iteration state and implement a small iterable or iterator correctly.

examples.title: Iterable and iterator protocols: direct language case
examples.situation: A custom Countdown iterable returns a new iterator for each for loop, letting two traversals progress independently.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A custom Countdown iterable returns a new iterator for each for loop, letting two traversals progress independently.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: An iterable supplies iterators; an iterator carries one-pass state and remains exhausted after StopIteration unless a new iterator is created from a reusable source.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because An iterable supplies iterators; an iterator carries one-pass state and remains exhausted after StopIteration unless a new iterator is created from a reusable source.

examples.title: Iterable and iterator protocols: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: Calling list on a generator iterator consumes it, so a second list call is empty and the source must be recreated for another pass.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: Calling list on a generator iterator consumes it, so a second list call is empty and the source must be recreated for another pass.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: iter(iterator) normally returns itself, exhausted iterators stay exhausted, multiple iterators may or may not be independent, mutation can invalidate traversal, and StopIteration has special generator rules.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Iterable and iterator are interchangeable, and calling iter on an exhausted iterator restarts it.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace iteration state and implement a small iterable or iterator correctly.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Iterable and iterator are interchangeable, and calling iter on an exhausted iterator restarts it.

analogy.example: a one-pass production route that can build a bounded result, expose an iterator, pause lazily, or wrap a callable can introduce Iterable and iterator protocols, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: StopIteration, suspension, close behavior, metadata, wrapper order, and memory allocation require exact Python semantics
